tables.caption: Comparison of Translational and Rotational Motion
headers: Rotational Motion about a Fixed Axis

subsections.title: Power in Rotational Motion
subsections.content_text: The instantaneous power $P$ is the rate at which work is done. Dividing the work expression $dW = \tau d\theta$ by $dt$, we get $P = \frac{dW}{dt} = \tau \frac{d\theta}{dt} = \tau \omega$. This expression $P = \tau \omega$ for rotational motion is analogous to $P = F v$ for linear motion. In a perfectly rigid body, there is no internal motion, so work done by external torques is not dissipated and goes to increase the kinetic energy of the body.

subsections.title: Newton's Second Law for Rotational Motion
subsections.content_text: The rate of work done is $P = \tau \omega$. The rate of increase of kinetic energy is $\frac{d}{dt}(\frac{1}{2} I \omega^2)$. Assuming the moment of inertia $I$ does not change with time (rigid body and fixed axis), $\frac{d}{dt}(\frac{1}{2} I \omega^2) = I \frac{(2\omega)}{2} \frac{d\omega}{dt} = I \omega \alpha$. Equating the rate of work done to the rate of increase in kinetic energy, $\tau \omega = I \omega \alpha$, which gives $\tau = I \alpha$. This is the rotational analogue of Newton's second law $F = ma$. Torque produces angular acceleration $\alpha$, which is directly proportional to the applied torque and inversely proportional to the moment of inertia $I$.

subsections.title: Example 6.12: Flywheel Dynamics
subsections.content_text: This example demonstrates the application of torque, moment of inertia, and the work-energy theorem to a rotating flywheel. It calculates angular acceleration, work done by a pulling force, and the resulting kinetic energy, showing that work done equals the energy gained.

worked_examples.label: Example 6.12
worked_examples.problem: A cord of negligible mass is wound round the rim of a fly wheel of mass 20 kg and radius 20 cm. A steady pull of 25 N is applied on the cord as shown in Fig. 6.31. The flywheel is mounted on a horizontal axle with frictionless bearings. (a) Compute the angular acceleration of the wheel. (b) Find the work done by the pull, when 2m of the cord is unwound. (c) Find also the kinetic energy of the wheel at this point. Assume that the wheel starts from rest. (d) Compare answers to parts (b) and (c).
worked_examples.solution: (a) Torque $\tau = F \times R = 25 \text{ N} \times 0.20 \text{ m} = 5.0 \text{ Nm}$. Moment of inertia $I = \frac{MR^2}{2} = \frac{20.0 \times (0.2)^2}{2} = 0.4 \text{ kg m}^2$. Angular acceleration $\alpha = \tau / I = 5.0 \text{ Nm} / 0.4 \text{ kg m}^2 = 12.5 \text{ s}^{-2}$. (b) Work done $W = F \times d = 25 \text{ N} \times 2 \text{ m} = 50 \text{ J}$. (c) Angular displacement $\theta = \text{length of unwound string} / \text{radius} = 2 \text{ m} / 0.2 \text{ m} = 10 \text{ rad}$. Final angular velocity $\omega^2 = \omega_0^2 + 2\alpha\theta = 0 + 2 \times 12.5 \times 10 = 250 \text{ (rad/s)}^2$. Kinetic energy gained $K.E. = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 0.4 \times 250 = 50 \text{ J}$. (d) The work done by the pull (50 J) is equal to the kinetic energy gained by the wheel (50 J). There is no loss of energy due to friction.

diagrams.label: Fig. 6.31
diagrams.description: A flywheel of mass M and radius R with a cord wound around its rim. A force F is applied to the cord, causing the flywheel to rotate about its horizontal axle.

sections.title: ANGULAR MOMENTUM IN CASE OF ROTATION ABOUT A FIXED AXIS

subsections.title: Angular Momentum of a System of Particles
subsections.content_text: The total angular momentum $\mathbf{L}$ of a system of $n$ particles is the vector sum of the angular momenta of individual particles: $\mathbf{L} = \sum_{i=1}^N \mathbf{r}_i \times \mathbf{p}_i$. For a rigid body rotating about a fixed axis, we analyze the contribution of a typical particle. The position vector $\mathbf{r}$ can be expressed as $\mathbf{r} = \mathbf{OC} + \mathbf{CP}$, where $C$ is the center of the particle's circular path on the axis. The velocity $\mathbf{v}$ is tangential to the circle and perpendicular to both the axis and the radius vector $\mathbf{CP}$.

subsections.title: Vector Analysis of Angular Momentum for a Particle
subsections.content_text: The angular momentum of a particle is $\mathbf{l} = \mathbf{r} \times m\mathbf{v} = (\mathbf{OC} \times m\mathbf{v}) + (\mathbf{CP} \times m\mathbf{v})$. The term $\mathbf{CP} \times m\mathbf{v}$ is parallel to the fixed axis (z-axis) and has magnitude $m r_\perp^2 \omega$, where $r_\perp$ is the perpendicular distance from the axis. We denote this component as $\mathbf{l}_z = m r_\perp^2 \omega \mathbf{\hat{k}}$. The term $\mathbf{OC} \times m\mathbf{v}$ is perpendicular to the axis. Thus, $\mathbf{l} = \mathbf{l}_z + \mathbf{OC} \times m\mathbf{v}$. Note that $\mathbf{l}$ is not necessarily parallel to the axis of rotation, unlike linear momentum $\mathbf{p}$ which is always parallel to velocity $\mathbf{v}$.

subsections.title: Total Angular Momentum and its Components
subsections.content_text: Summing over all particles, the total angular momentum is $\mathbf{L} = \mathbf{L}_z + \mathbf{L}_\perp$, where $\mathbf{L}_z = (\sum m_i r_i^2) \omega \mathbf{\hat{k}} = I \omega \mathbf{\hat{k}}$ and $\mathbf{L}_\perp = \sum \mathbf{OC}_i \times m_i \mathbf{v}_i$. The component $\mathbf{L}_z$ is the part of the total angular momentum along the fixed axis of rotation, and its magnitude is the product of the moment of inertia $I$ and the angular velocity

subsections.title: Components of Torque and Angular Momentum
subsections.content_text:
When discussing rotation about a fixed axis, only the components of external torques along the axis of rotation are considered. We can represent the external torque as $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \tau \hat{\mathbf{k}}$. The total angular momentum $\mathbf{L}$ is composed of a component along the axis $\mathbf{L}_z$ and a perpendicular component $\mathbf{L}_\perp$. Since the direction of $\mathbf{L}_z$ (along $\hat{\mathbf{k}}$) is fixed for rotation about a fixed axis, the equations of motion are:
$$\frac{d\mathbf{L}_z}{dt} = \tau \hat{\mathbf{k}} \quad (6.43a)$$
and
$$\frac{d\mathbf{L}_\perp}{dt} = 0 \quad (6.43b)$$
This implies that for rotation about a fixed axis, the component of angular momentum perpendicular to the fixed axis remains constant. Given that $\mathbf{L}_z = I\omega \hat{\mathbf{k}}$, substituting into Eq. (6.43a) yields:
$$\frac{d}{dt}(I\omega) = \tau \quad (6.43c)$$
If the moment of inertia $I$ is constant over time, the equation simplifies to:
$$\frac{d}{dt}(I\omega) = I \frac{d\omega}{dt} = I\alpha$$
Thus, $\tau = I\alpha$ (Eq. 6.41), which is the rotational analogue of Newton's second law ($F = ma$).

sections.title: Summary of Systems of Particles and Rotational Motion

subsections.title: Rigid Body Kinematics and Vector Products
subsections.content_text: A rigid body is defined as one where the distances between its constituent particles remain unchanged despite external forces. When fixed at a point or along a line, it undergoes rotational motion; otherwise, it may exhibit pure translation or a combination of translation and rotation. In rotation about a fixed axis, every particle moves in a circle perpendicular to the axis with its center on the axis, and all particles share the same angular velocity $\omega$ at any given time. The angular velocity $\boldsymbol{\omega}$ is a vector with magnitude $\omega = d\theta/dt$ directed along the axis of rotation. The vector product $\mathbf{a} \times \mathbf{b}$ results in a vector of magnitude $ab \sin \theta$, with a direction determined by the right-hand screw rule. The linear velocity of a particle in a rotating rigid body is given by $\mathbf{v} = \boldsymbol{\omega} \times \mathbf{r}$.

subsections.title: Centre of Mass and System Dynamics
subsections.content_text: The centre of mass (CM) of a system of $n$ particles is the point whose position vector is $\mathbf{R} = \frac{\sum m_i \mathbf{r}_i}{M}$. The velocity of the CM is $\mathbf{V} = \mathbf{P}/M$, where $\mathbf{P}$ is the total linear momentum. The CM moves as if the entire mass of the system were concentrated there and all external forces were applied to it. If the net external force is zero, the total linear momentum is conserved. The total angular momentum of the system is $\mathbf{L} = \sum \mathbf{r}_i \times \mathbf{p}_i$, and the total torque is $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \sum \mathbf{r}_i \times \mathbf{F}_i$. The fundamental dynamic equation for rotation is $\frac{d\mathbf{L}}{dt} = \boldsymbol{\tau}_{ext}$, where internal torques cancel out due to Newton's third law.

subsections.title: Equilibrium, Moment of Inertia, and Rotational Energy
subsections.content_text: A rigid body is in mechanical equilibrium if it satisfies two conditions: (1) Translational equilibrium, where the sum of external forces is zero ($\sum \mathbf{F}_i = \mathbf{0}$), and (2) Rotational equilibrium, where the sum of external torques is zero ($\sum \boldsymbol{\tau}_i = \mathbf{0}$). The centre of gravity is the point where the net gravitational torque on the body is zero. The moment of inertia $I$ about an axis is defined as $I = \sum m_i r_i^2$, where $r_i$ is the perpendicular distance of the $i$-th particle from the axis. The kinetic energy of rotation is given by $K = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^2$. The following table summarizes key rotational quantities:

subsections.title: Advanced Concepts and Clarifications
subsections.content_text: 1. Determining the motion of the centre of mass requires only knowledge of external forces; internal forces are irrelevant for CM translation. 2. The total kinetic energy of a system can be separated into the kinetic energy of the centre of mass translation and the kinetic energy of motion relative to the centre of mass: $K = K' + \frac{1}{2} MV^2$. 3. Newton's Second Law for finite bodies is derived from the laws governing individual particles. 4. The rate of change of total angular momentum depends solely on the total external torque. 5. The conditions for zero external force and zero external torque are independent; one can exist without the other (e.g., in a couple, net force is zero but torque is non-zero). 6. If the total external force is zero, the total torque is independent of the choice of origin. 7. The centre of gravity coincides with the centre of mass only if the gravitational field is uniform across the body.

subsections.title: 8. Relationship between Angular Momentum and Angular Velocity
subsections.content_text: The angular momentum $\mathbf{L}$ and the angular velocity $\boldsymbol{\omega}$ are not necessarily parallel vectors. However, for the simpler situations discussed in this chapter when rotation is about a fixed axis which is an axis of symmetry of the rigid body, the relation $\mathbf{L} = I\boldsymbol{\omega}$ holds good, where $I$ is the moment of inertia of the body about the rotation axis. This relationship is crucial for understanding the dynamics of symmetric rigid bodies rotating about their symmetry axes.

diagrams.description: A non-uniform bar of length 2 m is suspended horizontally by two strings. The string at the left end makes an angle of $36.9^\circ$ with the vertical. The string at the right end makes an angle of $53.1^\circ$ with the vertical. The weight $W$ of the bar acts vertically downwards at a distance $d$ from the left end.

items.problem: 6.1 Give the location of the centre of mass of a (i) sphere, (ii) cylinder, (iii) ring, and (iv) cube, each of uniform mass density. Does the centre of mass of a body necessarily lie inside the body?

6.2 In the HCl molecule, the separation between the nuclei of the two atoms is about $1.27 \text{ \AA}$ ($1 \text{ \AA} = 10^{-10} \text{ m}$). Find the approximate location of the CM of the molecule, given that a chlorine atom is about 35.5 times as massive as a hydrogen atom and nearly all the mass of an atom is concentrated in its nucleus.

6.3 A child sits stationary at one end of a long trolley moving uniformly with a speed $V$ on a smooth horizontal floor. If the child gets up and runs about on the trolley in any manner, what is the speed of the CM of the (trolley + child) system?
6.4 Show that the area of the triangle contained between the vectors $\mathbf{a}$ and $\mathbf{b}$ is one half of the magnitude of $\mathbf{a} \times \mathbf{b}$.
6.5 Show that $\mathbf{a} \cdot (\mathbf{b} \times \mathbf{c})$ is equal in magnitude to the volume of the parallelepiped formed on the three vectors $\mathbf{a}$, $\mathbf{b}$ and $\mathbf{c}$.

6.6 Find the components along the $x, y, z$ axes of the angular momentum $\mathbf{l}$ of a particle, whose position vector is $\mathbf{r}$ with components $x, y, z$ and momentum is $\mathbf{p}$ with components $p_x, p_y$ and $p_z$. Show that if the particle moves only in the $x$-$y$ plane the angular momentum has only a $z$-component.

6.7 Two particles, each of mass $m$ and speed $v$, travel in opposite directions along parallel lines separated by a distance $d$. Show that the angular momentum vector of the two particle system is the same whatever be the point about which the angular momentum is taken.

6.8 A non-uniform bar of weight $W$ is suspended at rest by two strings of negligible weight as shown in Fig. 6.33. The angles made by the strings with the vertical are $36.9^\circ$ and $53.1^\circ$ respectively. The bar is 2 m long. Calculate the distance $d$ of the centre of gravity of the bar from its left end.

6.9 A car weighs 1800 kg. The distance between its front and back axles is 1.8 m. Its centre of gravity is 1.05 m behind the front axle. Determine the force exerted by the level ground on each front wheel and each back wheel.

6.10 Torques of equal magnitude are applied to a hollow cylinder and a solid sphere, both having the same mass and radius. The cylinder is free to rotate about its standard axis of symmetry, and the sphere is free to rotate about an axis passing through its centre. Which of the two will acquire a greater angular speed after a given time.

6.11 A solid cylinder of mass 20 kg rotates about its axis with angular speed $100 \text{ rad s}^{-1}$. The radius of the cylinder is 0.25 m. What is the kinetic energy associated with the rotation of the cylinder? What is the magnitude of angular momentum of the cylinder about its axis?

6.12 (a) A child stands at the centre of a turntable with his two arms outstretched. The turntable is set rotating with an angular speed of $40 \text{ rev/min}$. How much is the angular speed of the child if he folds his hands back and thereby reduces his moment of inertia to $2/5$ times the initial value? Assume that the turntable rotates without friction. (b) Show that the child's new kinetic energy of rotation is more than the initial kinetic energy of rotation. How do you account for this increase in kinetic energy?

6.13 A rope of negligible mass is wound round a hollow cylinder of mass 3 kg and radius 40 cm. What is the angular acceleration of the cylinder if the rope is pulled with a force of 30 N? What is the linear acceleration of the rope? Assume that there is no slipping.

6.14 To maintain a rotor at a uniform angular speed of $200 \text{ rad s}^{-1}$, an engine needs to transmit a torque of 180 N m. What is the power required by the engine? (Note: uniform angular velocity in the absence of friction implies zero torque. In practice, applied torque is needed to counter frictional torque). Assume that the engine is 100% efficient.

6.15 From a uniform disk of radius $R$, a circular hole of radius $R/2$ is cut out. The centre of the hole is at $R/2$ from the centre of the original disc. Locate the centre of gravity of the resulting flat body.

6.16 A metre stick is balanced on a knife edge at its centre. When two coins, each of mass 5 g are put one on top of the other at the 12.0 cm mark, the stick is found to be balanced at 45.0 cm. What is the mass of the metre stick?

6.17 The oxygen molecule has a mass of $5.30 \times 10^{-26} \text{ kg}$ and a moment of inertia of $1.94 \times 10^{-46} \text{ kg m}^2$ about an axis through its centre perpendicular to the lines joining the two atoms. Suppose the mean speed of such a molecule in a gas is 500 m/s and that its kinetic energy of rotation is two thirds of its kinetic energy of translation. Find the average angular velocity of the molecule.
